design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore dungeons → defeat boss → resurrect world element → return to overworld → assist towns with resurrection progression. Real-time action combat with level progression and magic collection.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing action-combat execution against resource management (gems for equipment/spells, Magirocks for magic transformation). Environmental constraints in exploration (frozen wastelands, magma rivers) create traversal tension.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as Ark, a boy tasked with single-handedly resurrecting an entire dead world—progressing from barren underworld through surface resurrection (plants → animals → civilization → technology). Witnessing world transformation as direct consequence of player actions.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Tight real-time action combat with context-sensitive techniques (running/jumping/attacking combinations affect damage differently per enemy). No magic point system—instead, Magirocks must be collected and converted at shops into one-use spells, creating intentional friction in spell casting. Story progresses through world state changes rather than dialogue-heavy narrative. Difficulty notably punishing (per Wikipedia reception), suggesting designers prioritized challenging execution over accessibility.

difficulty.checkpointing: Checkpoint-like progression via town completion; game prevents backtracking to certain areas (e.g., Dragoon Castle becomes inaccessible after escape sequence), creating checkpoint-like progression gates.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited. Game is notably difficult with few difficulty settings documented. Player can grind levels and collect gems/Magirocks to improve stats and spell access, but core combat execution demands remain constant. No documented difficulty selection.

progression.upgrade_system: Level-based stat growth (HP, strength, defense, luck increase per level). Weapon/armor equipment progression via gem currency. Magic system: collect Magirocks → convert at shops into rings/summon medals → use spells (one-time consumption, Magirocks returned for reconversion). Boss defeats unlock new spell types.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Bosses and town tasks unlock new magic types. Chapter structure gates progression: Chapter 1 (Underworld, five continent towers) → Chapter 2 (Surface resurrection sequence: plants → wind → animals → humans) → Chapter 3 (Civilization development, technology invention, final conflict). Completing town tasks advances world state.
replayability_hooks: Multiple NPC side quests tied to town resurrection (hints of unused quest items: Aloe Leaves, Carrot, Airplane Parts suggest deeper town progression systems)
Magic ring/spell collection encourages replaying sections to gather Magirocks
Unused content suggests missed exploration opportunities (Dragoon Castle's inaccessible right-side areas)

world_building_techniques: Environmental storytelling: frozen wastelands, magma rivers, crystal mountains establish underworld atmosphere. Surface world barrenness communicates destruction scale.
Mechanical narrative: resurrecting world elements (Ra tree → plants → birds → wind → animals → humans) tells civilization evolution story through gameplay consequences
Character-driven arcs: Elle (Lightside princess) subplot, Beruga's cryogenic awakening and paradise ideology
Dream sequences: Elder communicates through dreams (Chapter 3), establishing mystical narrative layer
Cosmological worldbuilding: Hollow sphere planet with Lightside/Darkside duality representing God/Devil conflict across billions of years. Final battle in Antarctica sealed both realms.

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters tied to world resurrection narrative. Five towers in underworld each guard a continent; boss defeats unlock continental resurrection. Later bosses (Beruga) represent ideological conflicts. Designed as skill checks requiring execution of learned combat techniques (context-sensitive attack combinations).
notable_bosses: Ra (giant tree parasite removal—first major boss, gates plant resurrection)
Bosses of five continent towers (underworld progression)
Beruga (scientist with paradoxical immortality vision, final antagonist)

faction_indicators: Lightside vs. Darkside cosmic conflict (God vs. Devil forces)
Beruga's survivor faction with cryogenic technology ideology (pre-destruction elite)
danger_signals: Forbidden door in Crysta that triggers village freeze when opened—authority/taboo violation consequence
Beruga's Asmodeus virus plan (mass culling of 'insignificant' life for immortal zombie paradise)—eugenics ideology
Civilization development chapters hint at industrial conflict (though details sparse in provided sources)
environmental_storytelling: Underworld portrayed as frozen wasteland—visual metaphor for stagnation/death
Surface world's submersion into sea and underworld's sealing—cataclysm environmental evidence
Barren Guiana mountains, parasite-afflicted Ra tree—world sickness made tangible
Progressive town development (from barren to established civilization)—environment reflects player restoration work
Unused Heaven Statue item with 'summon rainfall' description suggests weather manipulation environmental puzzles were planned

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor (Terranigma page) - unused items, debug features, narrative development notes
Wikipedia (Terranigma article) - gameplay mechanics, narrative synopsis, character descriptions, release/reception info, cosmic worldbuilding
